questions.q: Walk me through how you'd estimate GPU memory for a training run before you even write a line of code.
questions.a: Weights in FP32 cost 4 bytes per parameter; BF16 halves that to 2 bytes. Gradients match the weight dtype, so add another 2–4 bytes. Adam is the expensive guest: it keeps FP32 copies of m and v, adding 8 bytes per parameter on top. Total for Adam in mixed precision: roughly 16–18 bytes per param. A 7B model needs ~112 GB before a single sample touches the GPU. Activations are the wildcard — they scale with batch size and sequence length, so profile empirically with batch size 1 first, then scale up until you OOM and back off one notch.

questions.q: You enable torch.autocast with dtype=torch.float16 and forget to wrap your loss in GradScaler. What goes wrong, and why?
questions.a: FP16's smallest positive normal is ~6e-8, so gradients with small magnitudes quietly flush to zero — your model trains but learns nothing, and loss may look suspiciously flat rather than NaN. GradScaler pre-multiplies the loss by a large scale factor before backward(), keeping gradients in the representable FP16 range, then divides them back before the optimizer step and skips the step entirely if any gradient is inf or NaN. On A100s and newer, just use BF16: it has the same exponent range as FP32 so no scaling is needed and the headache disappears. FP16 with GradScaler is legacy debt at this point unless you're on Volta.

questions.q: GPU memory limits you to batch size 4, but you want an effective batch size of 64. You implement gradient accumulation. What's the catch?
questions.a: Gradient accumulation runs 16 micro-batches of size 4, summing gradients each time, then calls optimizer.step() once — you get equivalent gradient quality at the cost of 16x wall-clock time per update. The silent killer: BatchNorm computes running statistics over micro-batches of 4, not 64, so your normalization statistics are noisy and accuracy can degrade without a single error message. Fix it by switching to LayerNorm, GroupNorm, or using SyncBatchNorm across ranks. Also a classic bug: calling zero_grad() before every micro-batch instead of only after the optimizer step — you throw away accumulated gradients and effectively train on batch size 4 the whole time.

questions.q: nvidia-smi shows 40% GPU utilization during training. Walk me through your diagnostic process.
questions.a: 40% GPU utilization almost always means the GPU is starving — it's waiting on data, not computing. Start with torch.profiler wrapped around a few training steps and open the TensorBoard trace view; look for long gaps between CUDA kernels on the GPU timeline. If DataLoader gaps are visible, increase num_workers, set pin_memory=True, and raise prefetch_factor — this alone fixes 80% of cases on Pune-grade servers with many CPU cores. If gaps persist, move heavy augmentations to GPU using torchvision v2 or NVIDIA DALI. If the kernel duration itself is tiny (model too small), increase batch size or fuse ops with torch.compile(). Paying A100 rates while running at 40% is just lighting money on fire.

questions.q: Interviewer asks: FP16 or BF16 for training — which do you pick and why? No 'it depends' allowed.
questions.a: BF16, always, on any hardware that supports it (A100 and newer, all modern TPUs). BF16 preserves FP32's 8-bit exponent range so you never overflow or underflow the representable range during training — no GradScaler, no loss-spike debugging at 3am. FP16's 5-bit exponent trades range for mantissa precision, which matters only if your activations live in a very tight numerical range and you're doing inference-style quantization tricks. LLM pre-training is universally BF16 now; using FP16 in a new training run in 2025 is a sign you copy-pasted an old config without reading it.

questions.q: You need to train a 13B parameter model on 4x A100 80GB GPUs. BF16 weights alone are ~26 GB. Lay out your memory strategy end-to-end.
questions.a: BF16 weights: 26 GB. Adam optimizer states in FP32 (m, v, master weights): ~104 GB — that's already more than a single GPU, so naive DDP is dead. Use FSDP (PyTorch-native ZeRO-3 equivalent) or DeepSpeed ZeRO-3 to shard weights, gradients, and optimizer states across all 4 GPUs: peak per-GPU optimizer memory drops to ~26 GB, leaving room for activations and batch. Add gradient checkpointing (torch.utils.checkpoint) to trade ~30% extra compute for dramatically lower activation memory — critical once sequence length goes above 2K. After this setup, profile one forward pass at batch size 1 to see real activation memory before committing to a larger batch. If you're still tight, reduce sequence length or consider 8-bit Adam from bitsandbytes to halve optimizer state size.

questions.q: Tell me about a time a training run looked fine on the surface but was actually broken. What caught it?
questions.a: Strong answer here shows you monitor more than just loss. In my case, a ResNet run plateaued at 72% validation accuracy — not embarrassing enough for anyone to raise an eyebrow. I was suspicious because gradient norms had flatlined after epoch 5, which usually means the model stopped learning. Digging in, a warmup + LR scheduler interaction bug meant the LR had dropped to 1e-7 within the first third of training and never recovered — the model was essentially frozen. The fix was straightforward; the lesson was not. I now log LR, gradient norm, and weight update norms (update norm / weight norm, target ~1e-3) alongside loss. If gradient norms trend toward zero early, treat it like a NaN — stop and investigate before wasting more GPU hours.

questions.q: What is data leakage and why does it make your model look great until it doesn't?
questions.a: Data leakage is when information that would not be available at prediction time sneaks into your training features, giving the model an unfair head-start. Your validation metrics look phenomenal, you ship confidently, and then production AUC falls off a cliff because that magic feature simply does not exist in the real world yet. Classic sources: using future data in time-series splits, scaling the entire dataset before splitting, or including a column that is a downstream consequence of the label. The rule is simple — your training pipeline must replicate exactly what happens at inference time, nothing more.

questions.q: You have a dataset with 40 features and suspiciously high accuracy of 98%. How do you systematically hunt for target leakage?
questions.a: Start with a permutation importance or SHAP summary — if one or two features dwarf everything else by an order of magnitude, that is your first suspect. Then check the Pearson or Spearman correlation of every feature against the label; anything above 0.9 deserves a hard look at its data dictionary. Ask whether that feature is generated before, during, or after the event you are predicting — even a 1-hour lag can be a proxy for the outcome in fraud or medical datasets. Drop the suspicious feature, retrain, and see if accuracy craters; if it does, you found your leak. Finally, do a temporal hold-out: train on weeks 1–8, test on weeks 9–10, and watch if that 98% still holds.

questions.q: What is the difference between data drift and concept drift? Give a concrete example of each.
questions.a: Data drift (covariate shift) means the distribution of your input features P(X) has changed, but the true relationship P(Y|X) is still the same — for example, your e-commerce model was trained mostly on desktop traffic and mobile usage has surged, so feature distributions look completely different. Concept drift means P(Y|X) itself has changed — what used to be a good credit risk is now a bad one after a macro-economic shock, even if the applicant's income and age look identical. Data drift you can detect with statistical tests like PSI, KL-divergence, or a simple two-sample KS test on each feature. Concept drift is nastier because label feedback is delayed; you typically catch it via a sliding window on prediction-vs-actual metrics rather than on features alone.

questions.q: Your model accuracy drops 15 percentage points two weeks after deployment. Walk me through the exact order you debug this.
questions.a: First, check if the drop is real — verify the logging pipeline and label computation have not changed, because a metrics bug is embarrassing but better than a model bug. Second, compare feature distributions between training data and the last week of inference data using PSI or KS test; if any feature has drifted significantly you likely found the culprit without touching model code. Third, check for upstream data pipeline failures — missing features filled with default zeros, a join that silently started returning nulls, or a third-party feed that changed schema. Fourth, if distributions look fine, pull a stratified sample of recent failures and manually inspect them — sometimes the world has just genuinely changed and the model needs a refresh. Only after eliminating data causes do you look at model internals.

questions.q: What are shortcuts or spurious correlations in ML, and why do neural networks love them more than humans do?
questions.a: A shortcut is a pattern that predicts the label in training data but breaks down under distribution shift — the model learns a proxy instead of the real signal. The famous chest X-ray example: models learned to predict pneumonia partly from the hospital name embedded in the DICOM metadata, because sicker patients cluster at tertiary centres. Neural networks are especially susceptible because they have enormous capacity and will happily overfit to any texture, token, or metadata artifact that co-occurs with the label. The fix is domain-aware data collection, hold-out sets that deliberately break the spurious correlation (geographical or demographic splits), and invariance tests. If accuracy drops drastically on a carefully curated OOD slice, you have found a shortcut.

questions.q: A junior engineer shuffled the full dataset and did a random 80/20 train-test split on a time-series fraud detection problem. Why is this catastrophically wrong and how do you fix the entire pipeline?
questions.a: Shuffling a time-series leaks future information into training: the model sees patterns from week 10 while predicting week 8, which will never happen in production. For fraud specifically, the model can memorise merchant-level fraud patterns from after the fraud wave you are trying to predict, giving impossibly good recall. The fix is a strict temporal split — train on T=0 to T=N, validate on T=N to T=N+k, test on T=N+k onwards, with a gap between splits to avoid label leakage from transactions still under review. Feature engineering must also be temporal: rolling aggregates computed only over past rows using a window function in SQL (ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW), never looking forward. Rerun the entire experiment; expect metrics to drop, which is the honest number.

questions.q: Design a lightweight drift monitoring system for a high-traffic prediction API serving 10M requests/day on a budget — no Evidently or expensive MLOps platform.
questions.a: Sample 1–5% of traffic reservoir-style using a Poisson sampler in your inference service and write feature vectors plus predictions to a Kafka topic. A small Spark or pandas job runs nightly comparing the sampled distribution to a reference window (typically training data or the last stable fortnight) using PSI for categorical features and a KS test for continuous ones — PSI above 0.2 or KS p-value below 0.01 triggers a Slack alert. Separately, track prediction distribution shift (mean predicted score, percentile buckets) as a leading indicator because label feedback takes days. When ground truth trickles in via a daily batch join in BigQuery or Redshift, compute AUC and F1 on a rolling 7-day window and alert on a 3% absolute drop. The whole system is under 200 lines of Python and a couple of cron jobs — no vendor lock-in, and it has saved production models in half a dozen companies I know of.

questions.q: Tell me about a time you discovered data leakage or a data quality problem after a model was already considered done. What did you do?
questions.a: Treat this as a story with stakes, a discovery moment, and a fix — not just a confession. A strong answer names the project context (recommender, churn model, credit scoring), explains exactly what the leaky feature was and why it felt plausible at the time, then walks through the impact assessment: how much did offline metrics inflate, and how badly did it affect production? Interviewers want to see that you caught it through rigorous process (temporal hold-out, SHAP audit, anomaly in feature importance) not just luck. End with what you changed in the team's workflow going forward — a checklist, a mandatory temporal split utility, a code-review gate on any new feature that touches the label column. Owning the mistake and shipping the process fix is the senior answer.

questions.q: Explain the bias-variance tradeoff in plain terms. Where does it show up in real ML projects?
questions.a: Bias is how wrong your model is on average — it systematically misses the truth. Variance is how much your model flaps around when you retrain on a different sample. A 50-node decision tree memorises your training fold and falls apart on the test set: high variance. Logistic regression on a nonlinear problem predicts the wrong thing consistently: high bias. In practice you feel this tradeoff when you tune regularisation — L2 on a neural net nudges weights toward zero, reducing variance at the cost of some bias. The sweet spot is rarely at the extremes; you find it by plotting learning curves and watching where train loss and val loss diverge.

questions.q: Modern deep networks are massively overparameterised yet generalise well. Doesn't that break the bias-variance story?
questions.a: The classical U-shaped test error curve breaks down once you cross the interpolation threshold — that's the double descent phenomenon. Beyond that point, adding more parameters or more data keeps improving generalisation, which sounds like magic but isn't: overparameterised networks have implicit regularisation baked in through SGD, early stopping, and architecture inductive biases. Bias-variance still applies, just not in the tidy textbook version where 'more parameters always means more variance.' The useful takeaway for production is that scaling model size alone does not save you — you still need enough diverse data, and regularisation choices still matter in the low-data regime every Pune startup actually lives in.

questions.q: What is calibration and Expected Calibration Error? Why should a senior ML engineer care beyond accuracy?
questions.a: A model is calibrated if, among all samples it assigns 0.8 probability, roughly 80% are truly positive. ECE measures the weighted average gap between predicted confidence and observed accuracy across confidence buckets — lower is better, 0 is perfect. You care because downstream decision-making uses the probability, not just the rank. A fraud model that says 0.9 when the true rate is 0.4 will cause your ops team to flood agents with false work or set a wrong threshold. Tree ensembles and SVMs are chronically overconfident; neural nets are often underconfident after softmax. Platt scaling or temperature scaling fixes this in an afternoon and should be a standard post-training step before any model ships to production.

questions.q: How would you detect and fix poor calibration in a binary classifier that's already deployed?
questions.a: Pull a held-out validation set, bin predictions into 10 equal-width buckets, compute the fraction of positives in each bin, and plot a reliability diagram — if it bows above or below the diagonal, you have a problem. Compute ECE as the weighted mean of those gaps for a single number to track. To fix it: temperature scaling is the fastest and most principled approach for neural nets — divide logits by a scalar T learned on the validation set, no retraining needed. For tree models use isotonic regression or Platt scaling. After the fix, track ECE alongside AUC in your model card and alert if it drifts on live traffic, because distribution shift can re-break calibration even when the ranking stays intact.

questions.q: When would you optimise for precision vs recall, and what does F1 actually measure?
questions.a: Precision answers 'of all the times I cried wolf, how often was it real?' — you care about it when false positives are expensive, like blocking a legitimate UPI transaction or flagging a doctor's note as spam. Recall answers 'of all the real wolves, how many did I catch?' — you care when missing a positive is costly, like a cancer screening or a fraud case that becomes a chargeback. F1 is the harmonic mean of the two, which penalises imbalance harder than an arithmetic mean — a model with 1.0 precision and 0.0 recall gets F1 of 0, which is the right answer. In practice, fix whichever business cost is larger by tuning the classification threshold, and use F-beta (beta>1 to weight recall) when the costs are asymmetric but both matter.

questions.q: PR-AUC vs ROC-AUC — which do you report for an imbalanced dataset and why?
questions.a: ROC-AUC measures the trade-off between true positive rate and false positive rate across all thresholds; because FPR has the huge negative class in the denominator, it stays small even when you produce a flood of false positives on an imbalanced set — so ROC-AUC looks flattering. PR-AUC plots precision against recall, and precision is directly punished by false positives regardless of class size. For a fraud dataset where 0.5% of transactions are positive, a model that flags everything as fraud gets ROC-AUC ≈ 1 but PR-AUC ≈ 0.005 — which is the honest picture. In Pune fintech or e-commerce recommendation interviews, always clarify the positive class prevalence before defending your choice of metric; defaulting to ROC-AUC on 1:200 data is a red flag.

questions.q: What are covariate shift and concept drift? Give a concrete example of each from an Indian product context.
questions.a: Covariate shift is when the input distribution P(X) changes but the label relationship P(Y|X) stays the same — your credit scoring model trained on pre-COVID salaried professionals now sees gig workers with completely different income patterns; the feature space shifted but fraud is still fraud given the features. Concept drift is nastier: P(Y|X) itself changes — a Diwali sale trains the recommendation model to associate high-spend behaviour with luxury items, but in January the same user's high spend signals restocking staples, so the mapping is different. You detect covariate shift via population stability index (PSI) on input features; concept drift shows up as prediction-to-actual divergence in your monitoring dashboard. Both mandate retraining, but concept drift also often requires rethinking features or the target definition.

questions.q: Walk me through how you'd build a system to detect and respond to distribution shift in a production ML model.
questions.a: At inference time, log every prediction with its input feature vector and timestamp. Run PSI or KL divergence on key features daily against a reference window — set hard alerts at PSI > 0.2 for any feature that drives your top-5 SHAP contributors. For label shift, track the predicted positive rate and compare it to your historical actuals with a delay-adjusted window once ground truth trickles in (e.g., fraud confirmed T+3 days). Automate retraining via a shadow model that gets trained on a rolling 90-day window and promoted when it beats production on a held-out recent slice. The missing piece most teams skip: version your reference window — after a major product change, reset it rather than comparing to stale history, or your drift detector cries wolf indefinitely.

questions.q: Why does cross-entropy work as a classification loss? What is it actually doing mathematically?
questions.a: CE loss is the negative log-likelihood of the correct class under your predicted distribution — minimizing it is equivalent to maximizing likelihood, which equals minimizing KL divergence between your predictions and the true labels. In plain terms: it punishes confident wrong predictions brutally (log(0) → ∞) and barely cares about already-correct confident ones. That asymmetry is exactly the signal you want — the gradient screams loudest where you are most wrong. It is also convex in logit space and its gradient with softmax is just (p_hat - y_one_hot), which is why it dominates classification.

questions.q: Your dataset has a 1:500 positive-to-negative ratio — fraud detection at a fintech. You see 99.8% accuracy and zero recall. Walk me through your loss-level fixes.
questions.a: That '99.8% accuracy' is your model predicting all negatives — a useless classifier. First lever: class-weighted CE where pos_weight = N_neg / N_pos; PyTorch's BCEWithLogitsLoss has a pos_weight argument for exactly this. Second lever: focal loss, which down-weights easy negatives with (1 - p)^γ so gradients concentrate on hard positives — γ=2 is a reasonable default. Third and most important: stop reporting accuracy immediately and switch to PR-AUC or F1 at a business-relevant threshold; accuracy on skewed data is a lie that hides the problem. Fix the metric before you fix the loss, otherwise you will not know if anything worked.

questions.q: Explain focal loss. When would you NOT use it?
questions.a: Focal loss adds a modulating factor (1 - p_t)^γ to CE, where p_t is the model's confidence in the correct class. When the model is already confident (p_t ≈ 1), the factor collapses toward zero and that sample barely contributes to the gradient — easy examples are suppressed, hard ones dominate. γ=0 recovers vanilla CE. Do not use focal loss when your dataset is already balanced (it just adds noise), when you have very few positives and cannot afford to down-weight any signal, or when your hard examples are actually label noise — focal loss will faithfully overfit every mislabeled row because it treats high loss as a signal of importance rather than a signal of annotation error.

questions.q: Your fraud detection system is evaluated on AUC-ROC by the business. You train with binary cross-entropy. How do you think about and close this proxy gap?
questions.a: The gap is real: BCE optimizes pointwise calibration (how close is p_hat to the label?) while AUC optimizes pairwise ranking (is a fraud transaction scored higher than a legitimate one?). Short-term: train with BCE because it is a good surrogate and converges cleanly, but use AUC on the validation set for early stopping and model selection — not BCE. Medium-term: consider pairwise losses like LambdaLoss or AUCM from the LibAUC library that directly optimize a differentiable AUC surrogate. Long-term: interrogate whether AUC is even the right business metric — at a bank, cost-sensitive recall at a fixed false-positive rate or precision at top-K often matters more, which opens the door to threshold-aware or cost-weighted losses that close the gap between the training signal and the actual business outcome.

questions.q: You have a multi-task detection model: task A uses CE loss averaging around 2.3, task B uses smooth-L1 regression averaging around 0.002. Training is unstable and task B never improves. What do you do?
questions.a: The scale gap means task B's gradient is essentially invisible — task A owns the optimizer. Fix in order of increasing elegance: (1) Manual loss weighting, L = λ_A * CE + λ_B * SmoothL1, tune λ_B to bring both terms to the same order of magnitude — this is what actually ships; (2) Gradient normalization — normalize each task's gradient to unit norm before summing, which is the GradNorm approach and requires no scale tuning; (3) Uncertainty-based weighting from Kendall et al. — learn log(σ_i) per task as a trainable scalar and weight each loss by 1/(2σ_i²) plus a log(σ_i) regularizer, which is theoretically principled and elegant but adds parameters and another hyperparameter to monitor. In a Pune startup you ship option 1 in a day; you mention option 3 in the interview to signal you have read the literature.

questions.q: Your offline AUC went up after retraining. Why might the A/B test still show a regression?
questions.a: Offline metrics are computed on a static held-out slice, but production has distribution shift, feedback loops, and a user population that responds to its own predictions. The held-out set was carved from historical interactions already shaped by the old model — survivorship bias baked right in, before you even touched the data. The model also never sees its own mistakes during offline eval, so it never learns to recover, but production users punish every bad recommendation with a click-away or a churn. Rule of thumb: offline metrics are the entrance exam, A/B is the actual job.

questions.q: Explain Goodhart's Law and give a concrete ML example where a team got burned by it.
questions.a: "When a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure" — in ML, the model finds shortcuts that game the metric without achieving the real goal. A fraud model optimized on precision starts flagging odd-hours transactions and ignores the actual fraud ring; a chatbot optimized on 5-star ratings learns to be sycophantic rather than accurate. The fix is a constellation of metrics so gaming one dimension blows up another, plus periodic human-eval spot-checks for the cases no automated metric catches. In India's startup ML culture, where a single north-star business metric dominates sprint reviews, Goodhart pressure is especially intense — protect yourself with at least one adversarial metric that penalizes the cheat.

questions.q: Your A/B test shows a statistically significant 5% lift on day 3. You ship it. What went wrong?
questions.a: You peeked — you checked the p-value before the pre-committed sample size was reached and stopped early the moment it crossed significance. The false-positive rate under repeated peeking can be 3–5x the nominal alpha, so that "5% lift" was likely noise that happened to look exciting mid-run. The correct approach: run a power calculation before the test (80% power, alpha=0.05 typically demands 2–3 weeks for most product surfaces with low baseline conversion), commit to the stopping criterion in writing, and don't interpret p-values until the sample is in. If early stopping is genuinely required by the business, use a sequential testing framework like mSPRT or Bayesian stopping rules — they're designed for this; your intuition is not.

questions.q: What is the novelty effect in A/B testing, and how do you detect and control for it?
questions.a: The novelty effect is when users engage with a new variant simply because it's different, not because it's genuinely better — the inverse (change aversion) works the same way in reverse. A new recommendation algorithm surfaces more diverse content; clicks spike week one as users explore, then decay back to baseline by week three as novelty wears off. To detect it, segment your treatment effect by days-since-assignment — if the lift shrinks monotonically over time, you're watching novelty decay, not genuine improvement. New users who never saw the old system are your natural control: if the treatment effect is large for returning users but near zero for new users, novelty is the explanation, not quality. The correct evaluation window for most content products is 2–4 weeks minimum before you declare victory.

questions.q: How do you build regression tests for a production ML model so a retraining run doesn't silently break things?
questions.a: A regression test suite for ML is a curated set of golden examples — inputs where you've pinned the expected output or score band — plus behavioral invariants like "similar inputs should get similar scores" or "output must not depend on name casing." Run these in CI on every model artifact before it's promoted to staging; if any golden example flips or any invariant drifts beyond a threshold, the deploy is blocked automatically, no human required. Slice evaluation by meaningful dimensions (language, city tier, device type, cold vs warm user) catches cases where aggregate accuracy looks fine but a specific segment quietly degrades — the failure mode that shows up in angry Slack threads, not dashboards. Think of it as pytest for your model: boring to write, invaluable the night a tokenizer version bump silently changes lowercasing behavior and your ranking flips for Devanagari queries.

questions.q: What does 'decision-driven evaluation' mean, and why is picking AUC as your headline metric often a category error?
questions.a: Decision-driven eval starts from "what decision does this number inform, and what are the actual costs of each error type?" — not "what does the leaderboard benchmark against?" For a loan default model at an NBFC, the question is at which threshold does expected profit (interest income minus write-off cost) peak, which immediately tells you whether to optimize recall or precision based on real rupee values, not abstract stats. This reframes AUC from a goal to a compass — what you actually need is a calibrated model, out-of-time splits (not random splits), and an expected-value curve as a function of your operating threshold. Two models with identical AUC can have wildly different business impact at the threshold that matches your actual cost structure, and the only way to see that is to tie eval to the decision, not the benchmark.

questions.q: Your A/B test shows no significant difference, but the PM wants to ship anyway. How do you handle it?
questions.a: First I'd distinguish "no significant difference" from "proven neutral" — these are not the same thing and conflating them is where bad decisions live. A null result with tight confidence intervals (say, true effect is between -0.2% and +0.3%) is actually evidence of neutrality, which can be a fine reason to ship if the change reduces tech debt or unblocks a roadmap item. But a null result with wide intervals means the test was underpowered — we genuinely don't know — and shipping is a coin flip where the downside is a production loss that's very hard to roll back culturally once it's the default. My ask to the PM: show me the CI width against the MDE we'd actually care about; if the test was underpowered I'd push to re-run it, and I'd document that position in the PRD comment before deferring to their call — because being right quietly doesn't help anyone.

questions.q: What is the difference between DDP and FSDP, and when do you pick one over the other?
questions.a: DDP replicates the full model on every GPU and syncs gradients with an all-reduce after the backward pass — simple, fast, great when the model fits comfortably on a single card. FSDP shards parameters, gradients, and optimizer states across all GPUs; each rank only materialises a full layer on demand via all-gather, then throws it away. The rule of thumb: if your model fits in one GPU's VRAM with room for activations, use DDP and sleep well. The moment you're doing 70B-scale work or squeezing a 13B onto 24 GB cards, FSDP is the sensible move. FSDP costs more communication per forward pass, so NVLink or InfiniBand matters more at that point.

questions.q: You're on 4 A100s with DDP and want to simulate a global batch size of 512 using gradient accumulation with 8 micro-steps. Walk through the correct implementation and the classic mistake that kills throughput.
questions.a: The correct pattern is to wrap all but the last micro-step inside `model.no_sync()` — this suppresses the all-reduce that DDP fires after every backward, so you're only paying for one all-reduce per optimiser step instead of eight. Also divide the loss by the number of accumulation steps before calling `.backward()`, otherwise you're scaling the gradient by 8× and your effective learning rate goes haywire. The classic mistake is forgetting `no_sync()` entirely: everything still trains, but you've done 8 all-reduces where 1 sufficed — on slow inter-node links that's a multi-second tax per step. A secondary trap is accumulating un-normalised loss and only dividing at the end; clip norms before that and you've clipped at 8× the true scale.

questions.q: Explain ZeRO stages 1, 2, and 3. Which stage do you typically reach for in production and why?
questions.a: ZeRO-1 partitions optimizer states across ranks — the thing that quietly eats 12 bytes per parameter for Adam. ZeRO-2 adds gradient partitioning on top. ZeRO-3 goes all the way and shards the parameters themselves, so no single GPU ever holds the full model at once; ranks all-gather what they need, compute, and discard. Communication cost rises each stage: ZeRO-1 is almost free; ZeRO-3 adds an extra all-gather in the forward pass. In practice ZeRO-2 (via DeepSpeed or FSDP's equivalent) is the production sweet spot — you recover most of the memory savings without the forward-pass communication overhead of stage 3. Stage 3 earns its place when you're training 70B+ models or you simply don't have enough GPUs to fit the model any other way.

questions.q: You are training a 30B model on 256 A100s rented as spot instances. Design the system so a node failure at step 50,000 costs less than 10 minutes of lost progress.
questions.a: First, checkpoint every 500 steps using distributed (per-rank) saves rather than consolidating to a single file — consolidation serialises writes through one process and becomes the bottleneck at 30B. Write shards to fast local NVMe, then async-copy to object storage (S3/GCS) so training doesn't stall waiting on slow remote I/O. Use `torchrun` with a robust rendezvous backend (etcd or c10d) and set NCCL's heartbeat timeout so a dead node surfaces in under a minute rather than hanging the whole job silently. On restart, each rank loads its own shard, the data loader seeks to the last committed step (keep the RNG state in the checkpoint), and training continues. At 500-step cadence you lose at most the forward time for 500 steps — for typical throughput that's well under 10 minutes even on a full 256-GPU job. Optionally layer in pipeline parallelism so a single node's failure only kills one pipeline stage and the rest of the ranks can idle and restart faster.

questions.q: Tell me about a time your large-scale training run crashed mid-way and how you diagnosed and fixed it.
questions.a: In a past run on 8 A100 nodes, the job hung silently at step 3,200 — no error, just stopped producing logs. The first thing I checked was whether the hang was NCCL-level (collective blocked) or application-level by attaching `gdb` to a stalled rank and printing the Python stack. It was stuck in an all-reduce, which meant one rank had died or diverged. Looking at the node metrics, one host had spiked memory and OOM-killed a worker quietly — the other ranks just waited forever for the missing participant. The fix was two-fold: set `NCCL_ASYNC_ERROR_HANDLING=1` so a dropped rank raises a Python exception instead of hanging, and add a watchdog process per node that pings a central heartbeat every 30 seconds and restarts the rank group from the last checkpoint on failure. After that incident I also switched from a monolithic checkpoint to per-rank shards so restarts took 90 seconds instead of 8 minutes.

questions.q: A candidate says 'LLMs work on words.' What's wrong with that, and what do they actually work on?
questions.a: LLMs work on tokens — subword units produced by algorithms like Byte Pair Encoding (BPE), which starts with characters and iteratively merges the most frequent pairs until hitting a vocabulary size target. 'Bangalore' might be 2-3 tokens; a rare domain term like 'collateralization' splits further. The practical implication hits your wallet: billing is per token, not per word, and a typo-heavy or transliterated-Hindi input can silently double your API cost. Always profile token counts before you sign off on cost estimates.

questions.q: Your Hindi-language chatbot is running 3x more expensive than the English one for the same word count. What's likely happening and how do you fix it?
questions.a: Tokenizers trained predominantly on English have dense vocabulary coverage for English but fall back to byte-level splits for Hindi — 'नमस्ते' can become 6-8 tokens where 'hello' is 1. This is called token fertility, and it directly inflates context usage, latency, and API cost. Fix: switch to a model whose tokenizer was trained on a balanced multilingual corpus — LLaMA-3 and Gemma handle Indic scripts significantly better than GPT-3-era models. Always run a quick fertility audit per language before quoting infra costs to the business.

questions.q: Why can't you just throw a 1-million-token document at a standard Transformer and be done with it?
questions.a: Self-attention computes pairwise similarity between every token and every other token — n² dot products and an n×n attention matrix. At n=1M tokens, that matrix alone is ~4TB in fp32; nothing runs that. Compute is O(n²·d) where d is the model dimension, so both memory and FLOPs blow up together. This is why context windows were stuck at 2K-8K for years and why every '128K context' claim comes with a performance and cost asterisk. Techniques like Flash Attention, sparse attention, and sliding-window attention exist precisely to work around this wall.

questions.q: What does Flash Attention actually do differently from standard attention, and why does it matter at scale?
questions.a: Standard attention materializes the full n×n matrix in GPU HBM (high-bandwidth memory), and the bottleneck is not compute but the repeated reads/writes to slow HBM. Flash Attention tiles QKV computation into SRAM-resident blocks and fuses softmax into the same pass, so the large matrix never lands in HBM — giving O(n) memory footprint and 2-4x wall-clock speedup. Flash Attention 2 and 3 pushed further with better warp-level parallelism and FP8 support. In production: it's default in most frameworks now, so if you're not explicitly enabling it you're probably leaving throughput on the table — verify with `torch.backends.cuda.flash_sdp_enabled()`.

questions.q: Explain KV cache to a backend engineer optimizing LLM inference latency. What does it store, why does it help, and what does it cost you?
questions.a: On the first forward pass, the model computes key and value matrices for every prompt token; without caching, each new generated token would recompute those for the entire prior context — O(n) work per step, growing as generation continues. KV cache stores those K and V tensors after the first pass, so decoding only computes attention over the single new token against cached context, cutting time-per-token dramatically. The cost is GPU memory: batch_size × seq_len × n_layers × 2 × d_head per element — on a 70B model with 8K context, that's tens of GB per concurrent user. This is why production serving engineers obsess over KV quantization (fp8/int8 cache), cache eviction, and prefix caching for shared system prompts.

questions.q: A fintech startup wants their LLM to know their internal lending policies. LoRA fine-tuning or RAG — walk through the decision.
questions.a: RAG wins when knowledge is factual, large, or changes frequently — policy updates monthly, so just refresh the vector store; you also get citation trails which compliance teams love. LoRA wins when you need to change model behavior or style: adopt domain jargon fluently, follow a specific output schema, or maintain a particular tone — weights encode behavior, not facts. For lending policies: use RAG for the factual content, and optionally layer LoRA for response format or persona. The classic mistake is using fine-tuning to teach facts — six months later the model confidently serves stale policy with zero self-doubt. Rule of thumb: LoRA for 'how it speaks', RAG for 'what it knows'.

questions.q: Why does an LLM confidently tell you a Python function returns a tuple when it actually returns None?
questions.a: LLMs are next-token predictors trained on text distributions — they generate text that looks statistically plausible given the pattern, not text grounded in actual runtime behavior. If most similar function signatures in training data had return values, the model fills in a plausible one. There is no internal 'check the source' step. RLHF compounds this: rewarding fluent, confident answers can bake in confident wrongness. Mitigations: add a retrieval step that pulls the actual docstring or source, or use a code-interpreter loop where generated code is actually executed and the output is fed back. For production: never ship LLM-generated technical claims without a verification layer — hallucination rate is a metric you measure, not assume.

questions.q: Tell me about a time an LLM feature hallucinated in production. What happened, and what did you change?
questions.a: The answer interviewers want follows a clear pattern: how you detected it (user complaints, eval harness regression, or golden-set failure rate spike), what the root cause was (no retrieval grounding, overconfident RLHF, or out-of-distribution input), and the concrete fix you shipped (RAG added, output validation layer, confidence threshold, or structured output schema to constrain the generation space). If you haven't shipped production LLMs yet, use a side project and be specific — vague answers get filtered fast in Pune ML interviews where everyone has a project or an internship story. The signal they're buying: do you treat hallucinations as a systems problem with measurable mitigations, or as magic that sometimes breaks?

questions.q: What is continuous batching and why does it matter over static batching in LLM serving?
questions.a: Static batching waits for N requests, runs them together, and releases all slots only after the longest sequence finishes — so a 10-token reply waits on a 2000-token one. Continuous batching (iteration-level scheduling) inserts a new request into the batch the moment any slot frees up, at each forward pass. In practice this pushes GPU utilization from roughly 30% to 70%+ and dramatically cuts latency for short requests. vLLM, TGI, and TensorRT-LLM all implement this. It is the single biggest throughput win you can get without touching the model.

questions.q: Explain TTFT and TPOT. What drives each, and which should you optimize for a real-time chatbot versus a batch summarization pipeline?
questions.a: TTFT (Time To First Token) is how long the user waits before seeing anything — it is dominated by the prefill step, which processes the entire prompt in one pass and scales with prompt length. TPOT (Time Per Output Token) is per-decoding-step latency — driven by memory bandwidth (each step reads all KV cache and weights) and active batch size. A chatbot is TTFT-sensitive: a 3-second blank screen feels broken. Batch summarization is TPOT-sensitive: you want total throughput, not snappiness. To cut TTFT, chunk your prefill or cache repeated system prompts. To cut TPOT, quantize weights and increase batch size so each memory read amortizes across more requests.

questions.q: Walk me through how you would quantize a production LLM to INT8. What are the real tradeoffs and gotchas?
questions.a: Post-training quantization to INT8 involves running calibration data through the model, computing per-channel weight and activation ranges, then clamping and mapping to 8-bit integers. In Python, bitsandbytes LLM.int8() is the fast path; for serious throughput, NVIDIA TensorRT or AMD ROCm toolkits compile fused INT8 kernels. The core gotcha: large LLMs above roughly 6.7B parameters develop outlier activation channels that blow up INT8 rounding error — LLM.int8() handles this with mixed-precision, keeping those channels in FP16 while quantizing the rest. You get roughly 2x memory reduction and 1.2–1.5x throughput gain. Always run a task-specific eval before you ship — summarization tolerates quantization much better than coding or math.

questions.q: Compare FP8, INT8, and INT4 quantization. When would you reach for each in a production serving setup?
questions.a: FP8 (E4M3 or E5M2 format) is hardware-native on H100 Hopper GPUs: roughly 2x over BF16 in throughput, minimal accuracy loss, and no painful calibration dance — it is the default choice if your infra has H100s. INT8 works on A100s, T4s, and consumer GPUs; it needs proper calibration but holds accuracy well for most tasks and is battle-tested in production. INT4 (AWQ, GPTQ, or GGUF) gives 4x compression but accuracy loss is noticeable on reasoning and math benchmarks — you reach for it only when memory is the hard constraint, like squeezing a 70B model onto two A100s instead of four. Decision tree: H100 → FP8, A100/T4 → INT8, GPU-poor or edge → INT4 with careful accuracy validation on your specific task.

questions.q: How does vLLM's PagedAttention work, and what problem does it actually solve compared to naive KV cache allocation?
questions.a: Naive serving pre-allocates a contiguous KV cache block per request equal to the maximum sequence length — a 4K context reservation sits mostly empty for a 50-token exchange, fragmenting GPU HBM like a bad malloc. PagedAttention borrows the OS virtual memory idea: it splits the KV cache into fixed-size physical blocks (pages), allocates them on demand as sequences grow, and maps them via a per-request block table. Multiple requests sharing a common prefix (same system prompt, for example) can point to the same physical pages — that is prefix caching for free. The result is near-zero KV cache fragmentation, 2–4x more live requests fitting in memory simultaneously, and directly higher throughput without any model changes. vLLM is essentially the Linux kernel's memory allocator applied to attention.

questions.q: You are designing a multi-model LLM serving platform on Kubernetes. Cold starts are killing P99 latency. Walk through your mitigation strategy.
questions.a: A 70B BF16 model is 140 GB; even at 7 GB/s NVMe throughput that is a 20-second cold start — unacceptable for user-facing traffic. Layer your mitigations: first, keep a minimum one warm replica per model (floor=1 in HPA), accepting the idle GPU cost as the cost of SLA. Second, cache model weights on the node's local NVMe, not fetched fresh from S3 each time — use a DaemonSet sidecar to pre-pull popular models to node disk. Third, load weights into CPU RAM (pinned memory) so the GPU transfer, not storage IO, is the only bottleneck. Fourth, move to a shared base model plus LoRA adapters: one always-warm 70B base, adapter swaps take milliseconds. Fifth, implement predictive scale-out — when queue depth crosses a threshold, start warming a new replica before overflow, not after. Instrument cold-start duration as a separate metric so you can spot regressions before users do.

questions.q: Your vLLM deployment shows TPOT of 40ms (fine) but TTFT is consistently 4 seconds. Walk through your debugging process.
questions.a: TPOT fine but TTFT slow means the decode phase is healthy and the bottleneck is in prefill. First, isolate whether the 4 seconds is actually inside the model or outside it — trace the full request path (tokenization, queue wait, network) before blaming the GPU. If GPU is the culprit, check nvidia-smi during a request: is utilization spiking to 100% for 4 seconds, or is the GPU sitting idle waiting on something? Long prompts with naive full attention are O(n²) — confirm Flash Attention is enabled; without it, a 2K-token prompt can take seconds on its own. Next, check if chunked prefill is disabled: one beefy prompt blocks the entire batch from generating tokens. Enable chunked prefill so long prompts are split across multiple iterations. Finally, check prefix caching — if every request starts with the same 1K-token system prompt, caching those KV blocks cuts prefill cost by however long the prefix is. Profile, fix one knob at a time, and measure TTFT at P50/P95/P99 separately since outlier long prompts skew the average badly.

questions.q: What makes a good text embedding model for RAG, and why can't you just default to text-embedding-ada-002 for every project?
questions.a: A good embedding model captures semantic similarity for your specific domain — ada-002 trained on general web text will flounder on legal contracts, Marathi loan documents, or internal JIRA tickets. The key axes are: token window (ada-002 caps at 8192, BGE-M3 matches it with better multilingual coverage), dimensionality (768–1536 is the sweet spot for recall vs. RAM), and whether the model was fine-tuned for retrieval vs. classification (MTEB leaderboard is your checklist, not vendor marketing). Evaluate on your actual data: pull 200 query-doc pairs, compute MRR@10, and pick the winner there — not on some English-Wikipedia benchmark. E5-mistral-7b and BGE-M3 regularly beat OpenAI embeddings on domain-specific subsets while being self-hostable on one A100. The 'just use ada-002' tax compounds every time you need data locality, offline inference, or a sub-100ms SLA.

questions.q: How do you decide chunk size for a RAG pipeline, and what's your tuning loop?
questions.a: 512 tokens is a popular default that's wrong for roughly half of real corpora. Long narrative documents — annual reports, textbooks, legal agreements — need 1024–2048 token chunks with overlap so reasoning units stay intact; short factual content like FAQs or structured tables can use 128–256 tokens without losing meaning. The tuning loop: pick 3–5 candidate sizes, run RAGAS Context Precision and Answer Relevance on 100 held-out queries, and ship the size that maximises both. Semantic chunking (split on sentence or paragraph boundaries via spaCy rather than fixed token counts) consistently cuts hallucinations by keeping natural reasoning units together. Always enable 10–15% overlap between consecutive chunks — it's cheap insurance against answers that straddle a boundary. Fix the overlap first, then tune size; don't do it the other way around.

questions.q: How does HNSW indexing work, and when would you trade it out for IVF-PQ or DiskANN?
questions.a: HNSW builds a multi-layer graph where the top layer has coarse, long-range connections and lower layers get progressively denser — think of it as a skip list for high-dimensional vectors. At query time it greedily traverses from the top layer downward, finding nearest neighbors in roughly O(log N) hops instead of brute-force O(N). The catch: HNSW keeps the full graph in RAM, so a 10M-vector index at 768 dims can eat 30–60 GB of memory. When RAM is the constraint, IVF-PQ clusters vectors into buckets and applies product quantization to compress each — you trade 5–10% recall for 8–16x memory savings. DiskANN is the choice when you need billion-scale search on an SSD budget; it keeps most of the graph on disk with a small in-memory cache. Most production teams (Qdrant, Weaviate, Milvus) default to HNSW because recall@10 above 0.98 is achievable with ef_construction=200, which is the number you should cite.

questions.q: Walk me through implementing hybrid search in a RAG pipeline. How exactly do you fuse the two ranked lists?
questions.a: Hybrid search combines a sparse retriever (BM25 for exact keyword matching) and a dense retriever (embedding similarity for semantic matching) — the magic is in score fusion. The standard technique is Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF): for each document, compute 1/(k + rank_in_sparse) + 1/(k + rank_in_dense), sort descending; k=60 is the canonical constant and it's surprisingly robust. Elasticsearch and OpenSearch have BM25 built in; Qdrant and Weaviate expose native hybrid APIs; for a DIY stack run rank_bm25 in Python and fuse offline. Hybrid consistently beats pure dense retrieval by 5–15% MRR on enterprise corpora heavy with product codes, names, or acronyms — exactly the data most Pune product companies sit on. The one query that crashes a demo is always an exact model number, and hybrid is what saves you. Don't ship a RAG product without it.

questions.q: What is a cross-encoder reranker and when does adding one actually move the needle?
questions.a: A cross-encoder takes a (query, document) pair as a single concatenated input and scores relevance jointly — unlike a bi-encoder that embeds query and doc independently, the cross-encoder's attention sees both token sequences together, catching subtle relevance signals that cosine similarity misses. They're 20–100x slower than retrieval but only run on the top-K candidates (typically 20–50), so the P99 latency hit is 50–200ms — acceptable for most applications. Reranking earns its keep when your retriever returns semantically similar but practically useless docs, which is common with short queries or noisy corpora. Models to know: Cohere Rerank, BGE-Reranker-v2-m3, ms-marco-MiniLM-L-6-v2 (fast, self-hostable). In practice a good reranker lifts NDCG@3 by 8–15% for almost zero code cost. If you've built a RAG system and haven't added one, you're leaving free recall on the table.

questions.q: Explain CRAG and Self-RAG. What problem does each solve and how do you pick between them?
questions.a: Vanilla RAG blindly trusts whatever it retrieves — CRAG (Corrective RAG) wraps a lightweight evaluator around retrieval that grades doc quality and falls back to web search when docs score low, giving you a safety net for out-of-corpus queries without changing the base LLM. Self-RAG goes deeper: the LLM itself generates reflection tokens (IsREL, IsSUP, IsUSE) inline during decoding, deciding per-claim whether to retrieve, whether the retrieved context is actually relevant, and whether the generated sentence is grounded — every sentence is individually gated. CRAG is simpler to deploy (one retrieval-grader, no fine-tuned LLM) and works well when you have a stable corpus with identifiable gaps. Self-RAG needs a fine-tuned base model that knows the reflection vocabulary, so it's more investment — but token-level grounding is gold for regulated domains like BFSI or healthcare. For most early-stage products, ship CRAG first, measure hallucination rate on 200 real queries, and graduate to Self-RAG if grounding remains the gap.

questions.q: Design an Agentic RAG system for a multi-hop question like: 'Which of our top-10 revenue customers in FY24 also had a support ticket escalated in Q3?'
questions.a: A single-shot RAG call dies here — the question requires joining structured CRM data and unstructured ticket records via intermediate reasoning steps. The architecture: an orchestrator agent (LangGraph or LlamaIndex's ReAct loop) decomposes the query into sub-tasks: (1) SQL tool to fetch top-10 customers from the data warehouse, (2) vector-search tool to find Q3 escalation tickets per customer, (3) code-execution tool if the join gets hairy. Run the per-customer ticket searches in parallel using asyncio to avoid serial latency multiplication — 10 customers × 300ms each = 3s serial vs 400ms parallel. The hard parts are loop detection (agents can spin on ambiguous tool outputs), cost governance (10 customers × 3 retrieval calls = 30+ LLM calls; cache embedding calls aggressively), and context-window budget (a 20-hop chain can overflow). Design tools with idempotency and max-retries=2, add a token-budget governor, and log every tool call for debugging — you'll need that trace on the first on-call incident.

questions.q: What is GraphRAG and when does it actually beat vanilla RAG?
questions.a: GraphRAG (Microsoft, 2024) ingests your documents through an LLM that extracts entities and relationships, builds a knowledge graph, generates community summaries at multiple resolutions, and then uses graph traversal alongside vector search at query time. Vanilla RAG retrieves the top-K most similar chunks; it fails when the answer lives in the relationship between documents rather than in any single chunk — 'What themes connect all complaints filed by Tier-2 city customers?' is a classic example. GraphRAG shines on global summarisation queries, multi-hop reasoning, and corpus-level insight questions. The cost is steep: indexing runs an LLM over every chunk to extract entities, which is 10–50x the compute of embedding-only ingestion; at 100K documents that's meaningful spend. Reach for GraphRAG when users ask 'why' and 'how' questions spanning the entire knowledge base; stick with vanilla RAG plus hybrid search for point-lookup 'what' questions. The Microsoft open-source implementation is the one to demo in interviews.

questions.q: How do you evaluate a RAG pipeline using RAGAS, and what does each metric actually tell you?
questions.a: RAGAS gives you four core metrics without needing labelled ground-truth answers: Faithfulness (does the answer contain only claims supported by the retrieved context — catches hallucination), Answer Relevance (does the answer actually address the question — catches off-topic rambling), Context Precision (are the retrieved chunks relevant to the question — diagnoses retriever noise), and Context Recall (did retrieval surface all information needed to answer — diagnoses retriever gaps). You run it with a test set of (question, ground_truth, answer, contexts) tuples; RAGAS calls an LLM judge internally, so it inherits the judge model's biases — use GPT-4o or Claude Sonnet as the judge, not a weak model. Faithfulness below 0.8 means your LLM is drifting beyond retrieved context; Context Recall below 0.7 means your retriever is dropping relevant chunks (tune chunk size, add hybrid search). Integrate RAGAS into CI: run 100 golden questions on every deployment and fail the PR if any metric drops more than 0.05. That's how you stop silent quality regressions.

questions.q: Tell me about a RAG system you built or designed end-to-end. What broke first and how did you fix it?
questions.a: The first thing to break is almost always retrieval quality — the team demos with naive 512-token fixed chunking, everything looks great on toy queries, and then real users type product codes or acronyms and retrieval returns garbage. Fix: hybrid search (BM25 + dense) plus a cross-encoder reranker, which recovers most of the recall. Second to break is latency under load — embedding + HNSW + reranker + LLM chains serially and P99 hits 4s; fix with async retrieval, query-embedding caching, and a smaller reranker model. Third is hallucination on out-of-corpus queries — the LLM confabulates when no relevant doc is retrieved; fix with a CRAG-style guard that checks retrieval score before generation and returns 'I don't have enough information' instead of a confident lie. A strong answer walks this arc honestly, cites actual metrics (MRR, P99, hallucination rate), and shows iteration — that's more convincing than claiming you shipped a perfect system on day one.

questions.q: Your PM wants the LLM to answer questions about internal company docs. Walk me through prompt engineering, RAG, and fine-tuning — and tell me which one you'd actually ship first.
questions.a: Prompt engineering is the fastest path: stuff context into the system prompt, costs nothing, ships tomorrow — but you hit context-length limits fast and pay per-token at inference on every call. RAG solves the scale problem: chunk your docs, embed them, retrieve top-k, inject into context — retrieval stays fresh when docs update and you never retrain. Fine-tuning teaches the model style, format, and domain vocabulary, not facts — it's expensive to run and goes stale the moment your docs change. For internal-docs QA, ship RAG first; only fine-tune if the base model keeps formatting answers in ways your users hate.

questions.q: Explain supervised fine-tuning in one breath: what dataset do you need, what loss do you compute, and what exactly changes in the model?
questions.a: SFT is standard cross-entropy on completion tokens only — you mask the prompt in the loss so the model doesn't waste capacity memorizing your question phrasing. You need (prompt, ideal-response) pairs; 500–5k high-quality examples beats 50k noisy ones every time, and 'more data' does not save bad labels. Every parameter in the model updates, so you need serious GPU memory or PEFT to make it tractable. The result is a model that has learned your format and task — but you've also opened the door to catastrophic forgetting of what it knew before.

questions.q: Walk me through LoRA mathematically. What is the actual update you're training, and how does QLoRA let you fine-tune a 70B model on a single A10?
questions.a: LoRA freezes all pretrained weights and adds two thin matrices A (d×r) and B (r×d) alongside each target layer — the weight update is ΔW = BA, where rank r is typically 4–64, cutting trainable parameters by 100–1000x versus full fine-tuning. It works because empirically, fine-tuning updates are low-rank: the model's knowledge sits in the frozen weights and you're nudging the subspace it attends to for your task. QLoRA goes further: quantize the frozen backbone to 4-bit NormalFloat (NF4), dequantize on the fly during the forward pass, and train only the LoRA adapters in bf16 — this cuts backbone memory by ~4x. Paged optimizers borrow CPU RAM to absorb optimizer-state spikes, which is what makes fitting a 70B model on a 48GB A100 (or two A10s) actually work in practice.

questions.q: You fine-tune a model on medical QA. Six months later the team notices it's bad at general coding tasks it handled well before. What happened and what are your three options to prevent it next time?
questions.a: Classic catastrophic forgetting: gradient updates on your narrow dataset overwrite the weight regions shared with general capabilities, because neural nets store multiple tasks in overlapping parameter space — nothing about SGD respects 'don't touch the coding knowledge.' Option 1 — PEFT/LoRA: freeze the backbone entirely, so general capabilities are physically untouched; this is the default right answer for most teams. Option 2 — replay: mix 10–20% general instruction data into your fine-tuning batches so the model keeps rehearsing prior skills. Option 3 — EWC (Elastic Weight Consolidation): penalize large updates to weights that were important before, using Fisher information estimates — theoretically elegant but rarely worth the overhead in production. LoRA plus a small replay buffer is the Pune production sweet spot: cheap, effective, and you can serve adapters modularly at inference time.

questions.q: Senior engineer says 'we need RLHF to align this model.' Junior says 'just use DPO.' Who's right and why — and when would you actually reach for the more expensive option?
questions.a: RLHF trains a separate reward model on preference pairs, then runs PPO to push the policy toward higher rewards — you're juggling a reward model, a frozen reference policy, a value head, and KL penalty simultaneously, and PPO on LLMs is notoriously unstable and GPU-hungry. DPO sidesteps the reward model entirely: it optimizes the preference objective directly on (chosen, rejected) pairs using a closed-form loss derived from the Bradley-Terry model, treating the LLM implicitly as its own reward. In practice, DPO matches RLHF quality on safety and style alignment with two training runs (SFT → DPO) instead of a four-component RL pipeline — the junior is right for most teams. Reach for full RLHF only when DPO plateaus, when you need a reusable reward model for other tasks, or when you want fine-grained multi-objective reward shaping that a pairwise loss can't express.

questions.q: You have a GPT-4 class teacher model that gives great answers but costs ₹5 per 1k tokens at scale. Design a distillation pipeline to get 90% of the quality at 10% of the cost.
questions.a: Step 1: generate a large synthetic dataset — run your real production query distribution through the teacher and save (prompt, teacher-completion) pairs; coverage of your actual query space matters more than raw volume, so cluster your real traffic first and sample from each cluster. Step 2: SFT the student on these pairs for behavioral cloning — you're teaching it to match the teacher's output distribution on your domain. Step 3 (if you have logit access): add a soft-label KL-divergence loss between teacher and student token probabilities — this gives richer gradient signal than hard token supervision alone, since the teacher's uncertainty over vocabulary is informative. Post-distillation, run your eval suite on both models: if the student drops more than 5–10% on your key metrics, either the student architecture is too small or your synthetic data has coverage gaps. This is exactly how Phi-3, Mistral, and most efficient open models are built — the teacher's synthetic data is the real moat, not the architecture.

questions.q: Walk me through the ReAct agent loop step by step. What keeps it running and what stops it?
questions.a: ReAct is Reason → Act → Observe, cycling in a single growing context window. The model reads the task, emits a thought (visible reasoning trace), outputs a tool call (action), receives the tool result (observation), then reasons again with all prior turns appended. Your system prompt plus the entire thought/action/observation history is the agent's only state — there is no hidden memory between steps. The loop terminates when the model emits a designated final-answer token, or when a max-steps guard you wrote kills it. Always set that guard; without it an agent that gets confused will politely reason in circles until your token budget is gone.

questions.q: Why do agents that work fine in demos fall apart on 10-step production tasks, and what do you actually do about it?
questions.a: Each step has some per-step error rate ε; overall success across n steps is roughly (1−ε)^n. At ε=10% and n=10 that is 35% success — most demos conveniently stop at 3 steps. The mitigation playbook: first, shrink n by giving the agent richer tools that accomplish more per call; second, add explicit validation nodes that sanity-check intermediate outputs before the next step proceeds; third, use structured outputs (JSON mode or constrained decoding) so tool-call parsing does not contribute its own error rate; fourth, checkpoint state after each successful node so a failure mid-chain restarts from there, not from step 1. You cannot fix compounding error by prompting harder — you fix it with architecture.

questions.q: Agents can use three kinds of memory. Explain each and tell me when you would actually reach for each one.
questions.a: In-context memory is whatever fits in the prompt window right now — zero latency, zero infrastructure, but it vanishes when the session ends and costs tokens proportional to history length. External memory is a vector store or key-value store you query at runtime — survives sessions, scales to millions of facts, but adds a retrieval hop and a retrieval failure mode. Parametric memory is knowledge baked into the model weights through pretraining or fine-tuning — zero retrieval cost at inference, but expensive and slow to update. A practical Pune-startup stack: in-context for the live conversation, pgvector on your existing Postgres for user history and document chunks, and parametric memory only if you are fine-tuning a domain-specific model that needs stable, frequently-accessed facts. Conflating these three is the most common agent-architecture mistake in interviews.

questions.q: Your team is debating LangGraph vs CrewAI vs AutoGen for a multi-agent customer-support pipeline with human-in-the-loop escalation. How do you choose?
questions.a: LangGraph is a DAG of nodes with explicit typed state — you define every edge, you can checkpoint any node, and human-in-the-loop is a first-class feature where the graph just pauses waiting for input. For any workflow that needs auditability, complex branching, or regulated outputs, LangGraph is the right call; the boilerplate pays for itself when you need to debug a production failure at 2 AM. CrewAI is higher abstraction (roles, crews, tasks) and excellent for rapid prototyping, but you surrender fine-grained control and debugging gets murky. AutoGen shines for adversarial or collaborative agent patterns — a coder-plus-reviewer pair, or agents that argue until they converge — but it is conversation-centric and awkward for state-machine-style workflows. For your support pipeline: LangGraph, because human escalation needs a deterministic pause point, not emergent behaviour from chatting agents.

questions.q: What is MCP (Model Context Protocol) and why should a team building internal agents care about it?
questions.a: MCP is Anthropic's open standard that defines how an LLM client connects to external tools, resources, and prompt templates via a lightweight JSON-RPC protocol. An MCP server exposes a catalogue of capabilities; any MCP-compliant client — Claude, Cursor, your LangGraph agent — can discover and invoke those capabilities without custom glue code. Think of it as USB-C for LLM tooling: build the connector once, plug in anywhere. The production payoff for a team: write one MCP server wrapping your internal APIs (Jira, internal search, billing), and every framework you adopt, or swap in, works immediately. It also enables runtime tool discovery, which matters when you want agents to dynamically load only the tools relevant to the current task rather than stuffing a 50-tool manifest into every prompt.

questions.q: How do you actually measure whether your agent is getting better across releases when there is no single right answer?
questions.a: Use a three-layer eval pyramid. Trajectory eval: did the agent call the right tools in a sensible order? Score this with an LLM judge given a rubric, or by diffing against golden trajectories you collected from human experts — this catches prompt regressions fast and cheaply. Outcome eval: did the final answer solve the task? For code agents run the test suite; for open-ended tasks combine LLM-as-judge with a 5–10% human annotation sample to keep the judge calibrated. Efficiency eval: tokens spent, wall-clock time, and number of steps — a correct agent that costs 10x more per task is not shippable in Pune startup economics. Beyond averages, track your failure-mode distribution — hallucinated tool calls, context overflow, infinite loops, wrong tool selection — because fixing the most frequent failure mode moves the needle more than nudging average accuracy by half a point.

questions.q: What is the difference between an offline and online feature store, and why do both exist?
questions.a: The offline store — BigQuery, Hive, Parquet on S3 — holds full historical feature values and is built for training batch loads; slow reads are fine. The online store — Redis, DynamoDB, Cassandra — holds only the latest value per entity and must serve sub-10ms reads at inference time. The real danger lives in the gap between them: if your online store computes a feature differently than your offline pipeline, your model silently degrades the moment you deploy — this train/serve skew is the number-one silent killer in production ML. The fix is a single feature definition that materializes to both stores from the same transformation logic, not two separate codebases that drift apart quietly.

questions.q: What is a point-in-time correct join and what goes wrong when you skip it?
questions.a: When building training data, you join labels (events at time T) with features. A naive inner join on entity ID can attach a feature value that was computed after the label event — that is data leakage, and your model learns from the future. A point-in-time join (also called an as-of join) gives each label row only the feature value that was available strictly before or at time T. In SQL this is a LATERAL or ASOF join; Feast, Hopsworks, and Tecton all implement it for you. Skipping it produces artificially high offline AUC that collapses in production — the classic 'works in my notebook, dies in prod' story that costs a sprint to diagnose.

questions.q: You wrote a new feature that needs 3 years of historical values. Walk me through how you backfill it safely.
questions.a: First, make the backfill job idempotent so re-running it after a failure does not corrupt the store. Run it on a separate Spark or Dataflow cluster so you do not starve the live pipeline. Write to the offline store first and validate immediately: null rates, value ranges, distribution shift compared to the last 30 days. Once offline data looks clean, materialise to a staging namespace in the online store and run shadow traffic against it before cutting over prod reads. Log the job's row count, start time, and end time — that audit trail is what saves you when a model trained on this feature starts misbehaving six months later and no one remembers the backfill.

questions.q: A feature column was renamed in the upstream pipeline mid-sprint. How do you handle schema evolution without taking down your ML system?
questions.a: Treat schema changes exactly like database migrations: backwards-compatible changes first, breaking changes never in-place. Adding a new nullable column with a default is safe — old consumers ignore it. Renaming or dropping a column is breaking; you version the feature (user_age_v1, user_age_v2), run them in parallel until every consumer has migrated, then retire the old one. A schema registry — Confluent Schema Registry, AWS Glue, or even JSON Schema files pinned in git — enforces compatibility at write time, not at the post-mortem. The nightmare scenario is renaming mid-training-run: half your batch reads old schema, half reads new, the model trains on mixed garbage, and you find out three weeks later when P95 latency inexplicably spikes.

questions.q: Tell me about a time a silent upstream data change broke your model or pipeline. What role would a data contract have played?
questions.a: A data contract is a formal agreement between the producer and consumer of a feature: it pins schema, value ranges, null rate SLAs, and update frequency. Without one, a producer silently renames a column or starts sending NULLs where values used to flow, and the consumer finds out weeks later when model metrics drift and someone starts a fire-drill. With a contract, the violation is caught at the pipeline boundary — Great Expectations assertions, dbt schema tests, or Pydantic validation at a Kafka consumer — and an alert fires in minutes. The behavioral story to tell: walk the interviewer through the debugging pain of a silent schema change (ideally one that cost real business impact), then explain the lightweight contract layer you added and how the next breakage was caught in four minutes instead of four days.

questions.q: Walk me through the four layers of ML monitoring. Why can't you just track model accuracy and call it done?
questions.a: Accuracy requires labels, which arrive late or never — so it's the last thing you see, not the first. The four layers are: infrastructure (GPU/CPU utilisation, memory, request rate, error rate), data/pipeline (feature distributions, null rates, schema drift — catch problems before they reach the model), model behaviour (prediction distribution, confidence histograms, fairness slices), and business outcomes (revenue, click-through, churn — the thing you actually care about). Each layer catches a different failure mode: a dead Kafka consumer blows layer two before layer three even notices; a silent schema change in an upstream table never raises an infra alert. You monitor all four because production failures are sneaky and rarely announce themselves at the accuracy layer first.

questions.q: Your model was trained on data from 2023. A PM says predictions feel off — CTR dropped 8% but accuracy looks fine on the weekly eval set. How do you diagnose and fix drift?
questions.a: When accuracy looks fine but business metrics are hurting, suspect concept drift or label contamination — the eval set itself may have drifted without you noticing. Start at the data layer: compute PSI (Population Stability Index) on every input feature; PSI > 0.2 is a red flag. Use a two-sample KS test for continuous features, chi-squared for categoricals, comparing last-7-days traffic against training baseline. If features are stable but output distribution has shifted, check for label leakage or upstream ETL bugs. Once you find the drifted feature(s), options in order of cost: retrain on recent data with a rolling window, add a domain-adaptation calibration layer, or fall back to a simpler rule for the drifted segment while retraining. Always version your reference distribution so the PSI baseline doesn't silently update itself.

questions.q: Your p50 inference latency is 40 ms, which looks great. Your on-call gets paged at 2 AM. What happened, and how do you fix it?
questions.a: p50 lying while p99 screams is the classic tail-latency trap — the median hides the 1-in-100 request that's hitting something expensive. Common culprits in ML serving: a single slow replica causing load imbalancer retry storms, GC pauses on a JVM-backed server, dynamic batching that occasionally waits too long to fill a batch, or cold-start on a freshly scaled pod that hasn't loaded the model weights yet. Diagnose by slicing latency by replica ID, batch size, feature cardinality, and request source — anomalies usually cluster. Fixes: cap maximum batch-wait time, use hedged requests (fire a second replica after 1x p95 and take whichever responds first), pre-warm replicas before they enter the load balancer, and enforce timeout budgets at the client with fallback to cached or default predictions. Track p99 and p99.9 in your SLO, not just p50 — you're only as fast as your worst tile your users actually feel.

questions.q: You've deployed an LLM API wrapper in production. What exactly do you monitor, and what's the first metric that tells you something is wrong before users complain?
questions.a: LLM monitoring has unique failure modes: the model doesn't crash, it hallucinates politely. Track token usage (input + output separately — output tokens drive cost), time-to-first-token (TTFT) and time-per-output-token (TPOT) as the two latency axes that matter for streaming UX, and output length distribution (a sudden spike means the model started padding or looping). Add a lightweight classifier or regex layer to catch refusals, empty outputs, and obvious hallucination signals (contradictions, made-up citations). Log every prompt-response pair with a request ID so you can replay failures. The earliest signal is usually TTFT degradation or a jump in output token count — both show up seconds after a provider issue begins, long before a user files a ticket. Set a hard output-token cap per request as both a cost guardrail and a hallucination circuit-breaker.

questions.q: Your LLM feature is burning $18k/month and your manager wants it under $5k. Walk through your cost-reduction playbook in order of effort.
questions.a: Start with the highest-leverage, lowest-effort moves first. One: exact-match caching — identical prompts (FAQ bots, template-heavy queries) are often 20-40% of traffic; a Redis cache with a 1-hour TTL pays off in a day. Two: semantic caching using embedding similarity (cosine > 0.95 threshold) catches near-duplicates — Pune customer support queries cluster heavily. Three: model downsizing — benchmark GPT-4o vs GPT-4o-mini or Claude Sonnet vs Haiku on your actual eval set; for most structured-output tasks the smaller model is within 2-3% accuracy at one-tenth the cost. Four: prompt compression — strip whitespace, shorten system prompts, use references instead of repeating context; input tokens are cheaper but still matter at scale.
